verses.text: Samuel dies, and all Israel mourns the prophet who anointed kings and judged the land for a lifetime. An era closes. Even the greatest of God's servants are taken in their time, but the God who raised them up does not die, and His purposes go on.

We meet rich Nabal — surly and harsh — and his wife Abigail, 'of good understanding, and of a beautiful countenance.' One household, two opposite souls. Scripture pauses to set wisdom beside folly, so that in what follows we may see which one God honours.

We meet rich Nabal — surly and harsh — and his wife Abigail, 'of good understanding, and of a beautiful countenance.' One household, two opposite souls. Scripture pauses to set wisdom beside folly, so that in what follows we may see which one God honours.

At sheepshearing, a time of plenty, David sends men to greet Nabal kindly and ask a share, having guarded Nabal's flocks. The request is gracious and fair. Those who have quietly served others may rightly look for a little kindness in return.

At sheepshearing, a time of plenty, David sends men to greet Nabal kindly and ask a share, having guarded Nabal's flocks. The request is gracious and fair. Those who have quietly served others may rightly look for a little kindness in return.

At sheepshearing, a time of plenty, David sends men to greet Nabal kindly and ask a share, having guarded Nabal's flocks. The request is gracious and fair. Those who have quietly served others may rightly look for a little kindness in return.

David's young men deliver the message in peace, asking only what Nabal can spare on a feast day. They are courteous to a fault, giving Nabal every chance to do right. God's people make their requests gently, leaving the response to the other man's heart.

David's young men deliver the message in peace, asking only what Nabal can spare on a feast day. They are courteous to a fault, giving Nabal every chance to do right. God's people make their requests gently, leaving the response to the other man's heart.

David's young men deliver the message in peace, asking only what Nabal can spare on a feast day. They are courteous to a fault, giving Nabal every chance to do right. God's people make their requests gently, leaving the response to the other man's heart.

Nabal sneers, 'Who is David?', and sends them away empty. Stung, David straps on his sword and leads four hundred men to wipe out the household. One man's rudeness almost provokes a great man into a massacre. Insult is a small spark that can light a deadly fire.

Nabal sneers, 'Who is David?', and sends them away empty. Stung, David straps on his sword and leads four hundred men to wipe out the household. One man's rudeness almost provokes a great man into a massacre. Insult is a small spark that can light a deadly fire.

A servant runs to Abigail, warning that disaster is fixed against them all, for Nabal is 'such a son of Belial, that a man cannot speak to him.' Wisdom in the household sees the danger the master is too proud to see, and acts before it is too late.

Abigail wastes no words; she loads donkeys with food and rides out to meet David, telling her husband nothing. Where folly blusters, wisdom moves quickly and quietly to make peace. A calm, generous hand can head off a calamity that angry words have set in motion.

Abigail wastes no words; she loads donkeys with food and rides out to meet David, telling her husband nothing. Where folly blusters, wisdom moves quickly and quietly to make peace. A calm, generous hand can head off a calamity that angry words have set in motion.

Abigail wastes no words; she loads donkeys with food and rides out to meet David, telling her husband nothing. Where folly blusters, wisdom moves quickly and quietly to make peace. A calm, generous hand can head off a calamity that angry words have set in motion.

Meanwhile David has worked himself into a murderous vow, swearing to leave not one male of Nabal's house alive. Even the man after God's heart, when wronged, can be swept toward sin. Anger nursed into a vow is one of the most dangerous things a good man can do.

Meanwhile David has worked himself into a murderous vow, swearing to leave not one male of Nabal's house alive. Even the man after God's heart, when wronged, can be swept toward sin. Anger nursed into a vow is one of the most dangerous things a good man can do.

Abigail falls at David's feet and takes the blame herself: 'upon me let this iniquity be.' She does not defend Nabal but owns the trouble and pleads for peace. Humble, self-forgetful intercession can turn away a wrath that no argument could.

Abigail falls at David's feet and takes the blame herself: 'upon me let this iniquity be.' She does not defend Nabal but owns the trouble and pleads for peace. Humble, self-forgetful intercession can turn away a wrath that no argument could.

Abigail falls at David's feet and takes the blame herself: 'upon me let this iniquity be.' She does not defend Nabal but owns the trouble and pleads for peace. Humble, self-forgetful intercession can turn away a wrath that no argument could.

She begs David to leave vengeance to God, who keeps him from 'avenging thyself with thine own hand,' and reminds him the LORD will surely make him 'a sure house.' Why stain a God-given throne with needless blood? Faith leaves its wrongs in God's hands.

She begs David to leave vengeance to God, who keeps him from 'avenging thyself with thine own hand,' and reminds him the LORD will surely make him 'a sure house.' Why stain a God-given throne with needless blood? Faith leaves its wrongs in God's hands.

She begs David to leave vengeance to God, who keeps him from 'avenging thyself with thine own hand,' and reminds him the LORD will surely make him 'a sure house.' Why stain a God-given throne with needless blood? Faith leaves its wrongs in God's hands.

Abigail assures David his life is 'bound in the bundle of life with the LORD,' and warns that revenge would one day grieve his conscience. She pleads he do nothing he will later regret. Wise counsel looks past the heat of the moment to the cost it leaves behind.

David blesses God for sending Abigail: 'blessed be thy advice, which hast kept me this day from... avenging myself.' He receives the rebuke gladly and turns back. The mark of a humble heart is to thank the one who stopped us from sinning, not to resent them.

David blesses God for sending Abigail: 'blessed be thy advice, which hast kept me this day from... avenging myself.' He receives the rebuke gladly and turns back. The mark of a humble heart is to thank the one who stopped us from sinning, not to resent them.

Abigail finds Nabal drunk at a great feast; in the morning, sober and told all, 'his heart died within him,' and ten days later the LORD strikes him dead. The vengeance David refused to take, God took in His own time. We may safely leave our enemies to Him.

Abigail finds Nabal drunk at a great feast; in the morning, sober and told all, 'his heart died within him,' and ten days later the LORD strikes him dead. The vengeance David refused to take, God took in His own time. We may safely leave our enemies to Him.

Abigail finds Nabal drunk at a great feast; in the morning, sober and told all, 'his heart died within him,' and ten days later the LORD strikes him dead. The vengeance David refused to take, God took in His own time. We may safely leave our enemies to Him.

Hearing of Nabal's death, David blesses God for pleading his cause and keeping him from evil, then asks Abigail to be his wife. The woman who saved him from bloodshed becomes his own. God honours the wise and humble, lifting them in ways they never sought.

The chapter notes David's other marriages, and that Saul had given Michal, David's wife, to another man. The shadows of David's house begin to lengthen even here. Scripture records the failings of its heroes honestly, that we may trust the God who works through flawed people.
